account.bio:
Uncensored, private, decentralized AI inference network.

CA: EmcxFTNVDqyLHp11NvwvLZ4D7LKGbG9i7B8RF7dwpump

followerSpikes.note: Account created Jan 2026; grew to ~4.7k followers alongside Virtuals integration announcement and viral sharded-inference demos. Likely organic event-driven spike rather than confirmed purchase.

followerQuality.notes: Sampled followers skew small and generic-crypto: many under 100 followers, several with 1-6 (e.g. @ARakhman2095, @Yondaime, @Eyu1349629). Consistent with a token-launch surge rather than a curated technical audience. No clear evidence of a purchased-bot spike, but quality is mixed and low-signal overall.

website.design: Polished, custom-built single-page marketing site (not a generic template) leading with the tagline 'AI powered by people, not data centers.' Uses custom iconography, scroll-driven animations, and themed feature callouts. Notably, the product is genuinely live and interactive: the web app supports Privy authentication, tier selection, and real-time streamed token responses via Socket.io. Consistent look across the main site, docs, blog, and treasury subdomains. Main drawback is heavy repetition of marketing copy on the landing page. ~78/100.
website.documentation: Strongest dimension (~80/100). No formal whitepaper, but well-organized, technically detailed docs built on Docusaurus covering What is c0mpute, Why it matters, How it works, Architecture, the $ZERO token, Staking, Referrals, Image generation, c0mpute code, API reference, and user/worker guides. Architecture described concretely with three components (user client, orchestrator, workers); orchestrator detailed as a Node.js/Socket.io central routing layer handling Privy auth, tier-based job queue, and worker registry. Worker types (e.g., browser workers using WebLLM/WebGPU) and tokenomics mechanics (70% worker / 30% network margin to treasury; 35% of $ZERO trade fees to treasury) are specified.
website.roadmap: Weak (~45/100). No dedicated forward-looking roadmap page with milestones or timelines. Progress is communicated retrospectively via the blog, which announces shipped features (e.g., c0mpute code agent live, auto-compounding staking, in-chat image generation, image API endpoint) rather than future commitments. Demonstrates a real shipping cadence, a positive credibility signal, but lacks explicit future planning.
website.teamInfo: Biggest warning sign (~20/100). No named team, founders, or company entity disclosed anywhere on the main site, docs, or reviewed evidence. Branding actively leans into anonymity ('owned by no one'). Only identity-adjacent signal is the GitHub handle 'leyten' in a source link. For a project custodying value via a treasury, staking, on-chain mechanics, and USDC payments, the lack of an accountable named team is a material risk.
website.notes: c0mpute presents far better than a typical low-effort crypto site: a live, working product; genuinely detailed technical documentation; a regularly updated blog showing real feature shipping; and a coherent custom design. The technical model (browser/native GPU workers, Socket.io orchestrator, WebLLM/ollama inference, credits-vs-$ZERO separation) is described with unusual specificity. Three skeptic's red flags temper the score: (1) a completely anonymous team despite handling staking, a treasury, and on-chain value; (2) no formal whitepaper and no forward-looking roadmap, only retrospective blog posts; and (3) the GitHub repo's contents could not be verified, leaving the 'open'/decentralized claims unproven. The buyback-burn + staker-yield narrative also warrants caution. Bottom line: technically credible and well-presented, with primary risks being anonymity and unverified open-source claims rather than the usual 'slick site, no substance.' Verify GitHub code, on-chain contracts, and team identity before trusting it with funds. Scores reflect website quality and disclosure only, not a code or financial audit.

github.activity: Actively developed but very young — ~16 commits on master with rapidly iterating README/claims over a span of days. Code is being escalated from a Qwen2.5-14B 2-GPU Phase 0 demo to documented 744B-across-7-GPU runs. No releases, no visible CI/test cadence, and no PR/issue community activity; activity is essentially a solo commit stream plus documentation evolution.
github.relevance: High. The code genuinely implements the claimed thing: pipeline-parallel LLM inference splitting contiguous layer blocks across GPUs on separate machines. Ships runnable Phase 0 code (phase0/node_kv.py, bench.py), an authenticated/encrypted pickle-free wire layer (ChaCha20-Poly1305 under SHARD_PSK), verifiable run receipts (docs/receipts/, docs/PROOF.md), and an honest roadmap that flags unsolved problems (activation privacy, NAT traversal). Not a fork, not empty, not unrelated — aligns with established EdgeShard-style research.
github.notes: Genuine, relevant, recently-active ML-systems engineering — a real working pipeline-parallel inference engine with reproducible Phase 0 code, encrypted transport, honest roadmap, and verifiable receipts. Star count (1) is honest, so no bought-star concern. Capped by a thin footprint: effectively solo (bus-factor of one), 0 forks, ~16 commits, no releases/CI/tests visible, and headline 744B-across-6-states performance claims rest largely on self-published receipts rather than third-party reproduction. Earned early external validation via a reported Virtuals Protocol integration. The crypto/token layer ($ZERO, c0mpute) is orbital marketing around legitimate code-level work.

developers.name: leyten
signals: Owner of the leyten/shard repo — the actual pipeline-parallel inference engine behind the project
Source of nearly all technical demo tweets (token-throughput milestones, shard protocol threads)
Has personal site leyten.dev and ~3.2k followers
Pseudonymous single-handle identity with no verified real-world credentials surfaced (context, not a red flag)
developers.qualityNote: Strong positive — a real, identifiable builder shipping verifiable code is the single best authenticity signal for this project.

signals: Credited by the account as author of an explainer video on the sharding protocol
Scope of contribution could not be independently verified
developers.qualityNote: Plausible technical collaborator/community contributor; low confirmation.

developers.name: leyten
signals: Sole identifiable core dev and repo owner (account id 86632281, ~13 public repos)
Linked X account @leyten with notable crypto-Twitter presence
Writes specific systems-level Python: custom TCP/QUIC transport, KV-cache pipeline, speculative decoding, ChaCha20-Poly1305 framing
Documents trade-offs honestly including unsolved activation-privacy problem
Other repos include solcord (TypeScript) and leyten (JavaScript); GitHub achievements (Pull Shark, Quickdraw)
Bus-factor of one — no second engineering contributor evident
Broader profile is crypto/social rather than a long ML-systems track record
developers.qualityNote: Clearly the technical author of Shard, producing credible and specific ML-systems engineering. Main limitations are single-contributor risk and a profile that is otherwise crypto/social rather than an established ML background.

engagement.cadence: Active and event-driven: mix of original posts and retweets of dev milestones (@leyten) and partner announcements (@virtuals_io). ~118 tweets since Jan 2026 launch.
engagement.notes: Original (non-RT) posts show healthy organic engagement vs a 4.7k base (e.g. 375L/84RT/84replies, 401L/80RT/87replies, 264L/61RT/38replies). High reply-to-like ratios suggest genuine community discussion rather than bot-liking. Momentum is real but small in absolute terms.

technicalDepth.notes: Among the highest-substance early crypto accounts. Claims backed by a public, detailed shard repo demonstrating pipeline-parallel inference of a 744B-param model (GLM-5.2, NVFP4) split across 6x RTX PRO 6000 over WAN. Repo emphasizes verifiable receipts (GPU UUIDs, public IPs, RTTs, output token hash) — a strong anti-fabrication signal. Progressive throughput benchmarks (~25 -> 30+ tok/s) and a live CLI/coding-agent product. Independent crypto press corroborates the pipeline-parallel approach. Note: the fully permissionless/decentralized swarm is an explicit future roadmap phase (Phase 3); current proofs are operator-run demos, so execution risk remains.

redFlags.message: Follower base skews toward small/generic crypto accounts (many <100 followers, some 1-6), typical of a token-launch surge. No confirmed purchased-bot spike, but follower quality is low-signal overall.

The fully decentralized/permissionless network (Phase 3) is still future roadmap; current demos are operator-run. Token flywheel (buyback-and-burn + staking) depends on real inference demand materializing.

summary: c0mpute is an unusually substantive early-stage decentralized AI inference project: its technical claims are backed by a real, public shard repo (leyten/shard) demonstrating pipeline-parallel inference of a 744B-param frontier model across consumer/prosumer GPUs over WAN, with verifiable receipts as an anti-fabrication signal. It has an identifiable lead builder (@leyten), a live website/docs/CLI product, and a confirmed integration with Virtuals Protocol \u2014 placing it well above typical pump.fun launches on authenticity. Main risks are the gap between impressive operator-run demos and a still-unbuilt permissionless swarm (Phase 3), a small mixed-quality follower base, and standard token-utility dependence on real usage driving the $ZERO flywheel."}
